title: Continuous machine learning on Euclidean graphs with unordered vertices

reviews.summary: This paper introduces the Nested Centered Distribution (NCD), a family of rigid-motion-invariant, permutation-invariant, and Lipschitz-continuous descriptors for Euclidean graphs with unordered vertices. The authors prove that NCD of order n (in R^n) is a complete invariant that uniquely identifies an embedded graph up to rigid motion and can be inverted in polynomial time; they further define a Nested Bottleneck Metric (NBM) that induces a Lipschitz-stable metric on the space of rigid equivalence classes. Empirically, the paper demonstrates a hierarchy of increasingly expressive invariants and reports fast, highly accurate element identification from local radial distances and separation of chemically different molecules in large molecular datasets.
strengths: The paper proposes NCD, a new invariant for Euclidean graphs that is complete up to rigid motion and computable in polynomial time for fixed n, addressing both unlabeled vertex permutation and geometric isometries jointly.
The construction is carefully nested (centered representations aggregated over anchors), enabling invertibility and obviating exponential enumeration over permutations or graphs.

The authors augment the invariant with a Lipschitz-stable metric (NBM) built from bottleneck matchings over max-metrics on centered representations, including a smoothing factor via triangle “strength” to handle orientation sign changes robustly.
The hierarchy SRD → SPD → PDD → NCD is well-motivated and practically useful as a cascade for efficiency vs expressivity.
Large-scale screening on QM9 and GEOM_drugs indicates that simple radial summaries (SRD) already separate many structures, and the full NCD provides stronger separation when needed.
The element-prediction experiment shows that very short-range geometric cues (few nearest-neighbor distances) are highly informative; the pipeline is simple and reproducible.
The problem statement is clearly formulated (Problem 1.1) with precise desiderata: completeness, Lipschitz continuity, invertibility, and computability.
Definitions are modular (SRD, SPD, PDD, CR, NCD, NBM) and the hierarchy is visually explained.
A complete, stable, computable invariant and metric for Euclidean graphs addresses foundational needs in geometric ML where permutation and symmetry pose persistent challenges.

The approach is broadly relevant beyond chemistry (e.g., pattern recognition for unlabeled Euclidean graphs) and provides theoretically grounded “coordinates” on the space of rigid equivalence classes.
weaknesses: Completeness for higher dimensions and degeneracies (e.g., all vertices confined to a lower-dimensional affine subspace) relies on appendices; the main text does not fully detail how uniqueness is ensured in measure-zero or fully degenerate cases without general-position assumptions.

The use of signed distances to encode edge presence is elegant but presupposes that graph edges are given and do not change under small perturbations; the behavior across bond-rearranging conformational changes or when edges are uncertain/noisy is not discussed.

The smoothing via triangle strength is key to Lipschitz continuity at orientation sign flips, but the geometric intuition and constants are largely delegated to prior work and appendices; sensitivity in nearly-degenerate anchors could be elaborated.

Empirical validation is mainly descriptive; there is no head-to-head comparison against widely used molecular descriptors (e.g., Coulomb matrices, SOAP/ACE/MBTR) on standard prediction tasks to contextualize benefits beyond uniqueness.

The very strong claim that chemically different molecules in QM9 and GD can be separated by a small number of farthest SRD entries is intriguing but under-documented: statistics, error rates, and robustness analyses (e.g., to coordinate noise, conformer variability) are missing.

The element-prediction baselines cited are drawn from different modalities/tasks (literature embeddings, composition-only models), so direct comparability is limited; a graph-geometry baseline with similar inputs would be more convincing.
Computation of NBM at higher orders can be expensive; scalability beyond screening subsets is not thoroughly analyzed, and wall-clock/resource details for the more complex stages are thin.
Some notation and terminology (e.g., “pseudo-metric L∞ on SRD” vs metric after sorting) could be tightened; occasional typos and formatting artifacts distract.
The boundary between point clouds (no edges) and Euclidean graphs (with edges) is sometimes blurred in exposition; clearer statements about what parts require edge information would help.
While the paper cites recent works on complete representations for point clouds, the empirical section does not compare to common invariant descriptors used in molecular ML in a controlled setting.

Additional discussion of invertible/factorizable graph representations and canonical forms (e.g., for 3D molecular graphs) would better situate the contribution relative to chemical informatics descriptors and canonicalization procedures.
reviews.questions:
1. Completeness under degeneracy: How does NCD handle cases where all points lie in a lower-dimensional affine subspace (e.g., planar molecules in R^3 or collinear point sets in R^2)? Can you provide a concise statement (and intuition) in the main text that covers these edge cases without appealing to general position?
2. Edges and stability: Since signed distances encode edge/non-edge status, what happens if the edge set can change across conformers or is inferred from geometry with thresholds? Is NBM stable with respect to small changes in edge sets, and is there a variant of NCD that degrades gracefully when edges are uncertain?
3. Complexity and constants: Could you report concrete wall-clock times and memory usage for NBM at orders h=2 and h=3 on representative subsets (e.g., m=30–100) and describe the practical breakpoints for using each level of the cascade?
4. Empirical evidence on “distinguishability”: The claim that a handful of farthest SRD entries separates all distinct compositions in QM9/GD is striking. Can you provide summary statistics (number of collisions, exact m and rounding precision required) and a small failure analysis if any?
5. Baselines and tasks: Can you include a controlled comparison against standard molecular geometry descriptors (e.g., Coulomb matrices, SOAP/ACE) on a simple recognition or retrieval task to quantify the practical advantage of NCD/ NBMs under noise and permutations?
6. 3D completeness in the main text: Since applications are primarily 3D, could you move a concise version of the n=3 completeness/invertibility/stability statements from the appendix into the main body, including the minimal anchor order and any special handling for mirror images?
7. Code and reproducibility: Will you release code and precomputed invariants/metrics for QM9 and GEOM_drugs to facilitate adoption and verification?
reviews.assessment:
This paper tackles a fundamental and under-served problem in geometric machine learning for Euclidean graphs with unordered vertices: constructing a complete, Lipschitz-stable, and computable invariant with an associated metric. The proposed Nested Centered Distribution and Nested Bottleneck Metric satisfy these desiderata (under the provided theorems), generalize earlier work beyond point clouds to graphs with edges, and are organized in a practical cascade that balances efficiency and expressivity. The theoretical contribution is strong and, if proofs hold as stated, of clear significance.

On the empirical side, the demonstrations are promising but limited. The striking claims about separability using very small subsets of SRD coordinates merit a deeper quantitative substantiation, and comparisons to standard geometric descriptors on benchmark tasks would strengthen the case for impact at ICML. Some treatment of degeneracies and edge uncertainty in the main text would also improve clarity and confidence.

Overall, I view this as a theoretically substantial contribution with high potential value to the ICML community, especially given the importance of invariant, stable representations for learning on geometric data. I recommend acceptance contingent on bolstering the empirical evaluation and clarifying edge/degeneracy handling, but I acknowledge that the current experimental section is lighter than typical ICML standards.
